Job's friends were silenced, but they were not convinced. Others had been present. Elihu was justly displeased with Job, because he seemed more concerned about his own reputation than about God’s justice and goodness. Elihu was displeased with Job's friends because they had not been honest with Job. Rarely is a quarrel started, and even less often is it continued, without faults on both sides. Those who seek truth must not reject what is true and good from either side, nor approve or defend what is wrong.

Elihu claimed to speak by the inspiration of the Holy Spirit, and he corrected both parties. He allowed that those with the most experience should speak first. But God gives wisdom as he chooses; this encouraged him to state his opinion. By paying attention to the word of God and depending on the Holy Spirit, young people may become wiser than older people; but this wisdom will make them quick to listen, slow to speak, and willing to give others a patient hearing.

Even if we are sure that the Spirit of God suggested what we are about to say, we still ought to wait until it is our turn to speak. God is a God of order, not of confusion. It is great refreshment for a good person to speak for the glory of the Lord and to build up others. And the more we consider the majesty of God as our Maker, and the more we fear his wrath and justice, the less we will act out of sinful fear or flattery toward people. If we could always keep the Lord’s wrath before us, in his mercies and his terrors, we would not be moved from doing our duty in whatever we are called to do.
